title: Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) and Minimum Bactericidal Concentration (MBC) Assays Using Broth Microdilution Method
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"93dmp","text":"The presented protocol outlines a comprehensive assessment of the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) values for bacterial cell cultures. These experiments are vital for screening bacterial susceptibility to antibiotics and substances with potential antibacterial properties. The protocol not only covers the necessary preparatory steps but also introduces the application of the widely recognized Gompertz model. The protocol ensures a smooth execution of the assessment through thorough preparation and step-by-step instructions. The user-friendly instructions provided enable researchers to easily follow the protocol, facilitating the implementation of the assessment. By adhering to the outlined procedures, researchers can acquire a deeper understanding of bacterial susceptibility, evaluate the efficacy of antimicrobial agents through MIC and MBC values, and contribute to the advancement of antibacterial strategies.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"4p75f","text":"The following protocol is optimised for substances that are soluble in water. However, we recommend to prepare a small portion of the solution of the investigated substance and mix it with three potions of Mueller-Hinton Broth medium to see if any precipitation or liquid-liquid phase separation occurs. If that is the case – addition of small amount of DMSO (up to 1%) may help to solubilise the substance. If this won't help, we recommend using different technique to assess MIC and MBC values.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cfbl","text":"","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"72mfc","text":"The following media have been used in the protocol:","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bhj4u","text":"   – MHB","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"d9p6s","text":"   – MHA","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"cmud6","text":"","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"722bc","text":"The protocol was optimised and tested using following strains:","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"eb0dg","text":"S. aureus 43300 (ATCC)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bt4mc","text":"P. aeruginosa 15442 (ATCC)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cj20","text":"E. coli K-12 (ATCC)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7r5mm","text":"","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"145u9","text":"This protocol can be easily adapted to other strains of bacteria, however, make sure to use suitable media, if the investigated strain is not growing on the MHA/MHB. Bare in mind, that use of MHA/MHB is in accordance with CLSI guidelines; hence, any changes have to be indicated when publishing the results.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c0iff","text":"","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f1cec","text":"Gompertz model file was adapted from OriginPro forums and was originally posted by the user YimingChen.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"reagents","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"id":959712,"guid":"437A3E91341F11EE9D4302C0B41BC903","name":"BD DIFCO™ Mueller Hinton Broth 500g","mol_weight":0,"sku":"275730","url":"https://www.bd.com/en-us/products-and-solutions/products/product-page.275730#overview","cas_number":"","rrid":"","vendor":{"id":1503,"name":"Becton Dickinson (BD)","link":"https://www.bd.com/en-us","is_requested":false,"image":{"source":"/img/vendors/placeholder.png","placeholder":"/img/vendors/placeholder.png"}},"is_public":true,"can_edit":false}},"1":{"type":"reagents","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"id":959713,"guid":"437A3EE3341F11EE9D4302C0B41BC903","name":"BD DIFCO™ Mueller Hinton Agar 500g","mol_weight":0,"sku":"225250","url":"https://www.bd.com/en-us/products-and-solutions/products/product-page.225250#overview","cas_number":"","rrid":"","vendor":{"id":1503,"name":"Becton Dickinson (BD)","link":"https://www.bd.com/en-us","is_requested":false,"image":{"source":"/img/vendors/placeholder.png","placeholder":"/img/vendors/placeholder.png"}},"is_public":true,"can_edit":false}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"fghfs","text":"Be sure to read the protocol fully, before starting any manipulation. Be sure that all the equipment needed is working properly and you have enough time to do all the manipulations. If it is your first time doing this, be sure to plan more time, as usually first times take longer. Be sure that both you and environment is well protected and your laboratory regulations allow for manipulation with bacteria.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3blar","text":"","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2dhn9","text":"For a single test, that consist of a triplicate you will need around 4 mL of MHB, however, it is recommended to have much more prepared (preferably around 20 mL) in case any mishaps occur. You have to have an inoculated medium on a Petri dish with discrete colonies already prepared.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"ejvmb","text":"All the manipulations with handling live bacteria should be executed using the biosafety cabinet at all times unless local regulations state otherwise.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"3b58l","text":"Standard 96-well microplate","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8ek7f","text":"Pipettes","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fmut5","text":"Square Petri dishes with MHA","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"310el","text":"Microtube (a.k.a EppendorfTube®)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":30,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3da6k","text":"Trough (optional)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3juru","text":"Multichannel pipette (optional)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4jqru","text":"Sealing safety film for microplate (optional)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Take the inoculated Petri dish and transfer one colony to a tube with approx. 3 mL of fresh MHB .

Incubate the tube for around 03:00:00 (or more, depending on the strain and the size and age of the colony used) at 37 °C . Stock colony can be prepared in the morning and be worked with in the early afternoon. It can also grow overnight (to the late stationary phase) and be diluted (at least by 1:50 or 1:100) with the fresh MHB . In case of the second scenario, it will take 2-3 h to reach the optimum OD 600 (around 0.1). If you have any concerns refer to CLSI M100 guidelines.

This part should be done only when the 96-well microplate is ready to be inoculated. Prepare standardized inoculum by first measuring the OD 600 of your stock colony. If the value of the OD 600 : Is greater than 0.1, dilute the culture with MHB to reach a value of 0.1, Is between 0.09 and 0.1 you can move to a next step, is below 0.09 put the culture back and incubate it for 15-30 minutes more.
Pour 10 mL of MHB into a trough and add 100 µL of the standardized inoculum . Mix it by flushing couple of times with a pipette. 10 mL of diluted standardized inoculum is enough to inoculate 16 rows.

Pour around 10 mL of MHB to the trough and: Add 50 µL of MHB to each well in columns 1-10 (growth control, GC ( 1 )+ serial dilution ( 2-10 )), Leave wells in column 11 empty (highest concentration of serial dilution), Add 100 µL to the wells in column 12 (sterility control, SC ). Typical microplate layout for this step.

Dissolve tested substance ( X ) in MHB at twice the maximum concentration for the test. E.g., if you want to test the concentration series of X starting from 100 µg/mL then you need to prepare the stock solution of tested substance in the concentration of 200 µg/mL . Adjust the total volume of dilution to your needs. A 100 µL is needed for each row.

Add 100 µL of the just prepared solution of X diluted in MHB to the wells in column 11 and remove and pass 50 µL to next well until reaching wells in column 2 , This will result in the wells in column 2 having 100 µL . Remove 50 µL of the liquid content from these wells and discard it. Serial dilution of X on the microplate.

Using multichannel pipette add 50 µL of diluted standardized inoculum to each well of columns 1-11 . For more information go to step #3 . The standardized inoculum is equivalent to 10^8 CFU/mL . After 1:100 dilution in MHB the concentration is 10^6 CFU/mL . Taking 50 µL of the diluted bacterial suspension and adding it to all the wells of columns 1-11 results in a final concentration of approx. 5×10^5 CFU/mL . Be sure that the time needed to prepare and dispense the OD 600 -adjusted bacteria solution do not exceed 00:15:00 . Addition of the diluted standardized inoculum
Put a protective film on that-prepared well plate. Cover it with a lid. Put a name and/or other details on the side of the plate. Avoid leaving any marks on the lid.
After the incubation take the microplate out of the incubator. Remove the lid from the plate and protective film if applied and let it cool down in Room temperature for 00:15:00 .
After that time put the microplate into the plate reader and read OD 600 values of the wells' content.

Gather the data, and using plotting program such as Prism, OriginPro, Excel or Kaleida plot the data and fit it using modified Gompertz model. MIC value lies on the intersection of the lower part of the jump with the jump slope ( example ). A OriginPro model file can be accessed here: Gompertz - MIC.fdf Alternatively – MIC value can be assessed visually. In that case MIC value is where the no turbidity is observed. Example of data modelled using Gompertz model (OriginPro). Visual assessment of MIC value
Transfer 100 µL of the content of the wells were no turbidity was observed to a separate 96-well microplate and dilute it ten fold serially with the solution of NaCl 9 g/L
Plate 20 µL of the content of the wells directly onto square Petri dish/dishes with MHA and incubate Overnight Possible maximum plating area of square Petri dish (side of. ca 120 mm).

Next morning count the colonies on the plates. You can omit the dilution and counting, and directly plate 20 µL of the content of non-turbid wells after MIC assessment. MBC value is, where no growth was observed. You can use this CFU calculator to count the surviving colonies that are between MIC and MBC values: CFU counter_ver_6.xlsm
creator: Tomasz Swebocki
